차가운 거 드릴까요? (Would you like a cold one?)

vocabulary.english: Consonant: r/l (between English L and R)
Vowel sound: oh (jaw dropped, lips rounded)
Vowel sound: oo (lips pursed, jaw dropped)
Vowel sound: eu (unique Korean sound, jaw NOT dropped)
Side dishes (served with rice, unlimited free refills)

vocabulary.example: 노래 들어요 (I listen to a song)
가게에 갔어요 (I went to the store)
갱신된 여권과 무제한 여행을 받고 싶어요.
한국 사람들은 이런 사람을 '똑똑한 사람'이라고 해요.
찬 밥 (cold rice), 찬 물 (cold water)

patterns.pattern: H-Drop Rule: 하 → 아 (in natural speech)
patterns.meaning: Drop the H sound in 하 for more natural, native-sounding speech
Polite request pattern: 'Please give me [quantity] of [object]'
Please go to [place] (used in taxi context)
When a syllable ending consonant is followed by a vowel-starting syllable, the consonant links to the next syllable
May I [action]? / Is it okay to [action]? (permission request)
Where is [noun]?
Softened polite request: 'Could I please have [object]' (좀 adds politeness)
I will eat at [place]
It's okay / I'm fine / No thank you. Rising pitch = question (Is it okay?)

examples.english: It's okay / I'm fine / No thank you (statement)
Is it okay? / Are you okay? (question, rising pitch)

patterns.meaning: I will pay by card (instrument particle 로 + future intent 할게요)
Request for takeout/to-go: 'Please wrap it up'

examples.english: To-go please / Wrap it up please

patterns.meaning: Do you have [noun]? / There is no [noun]. Used for existence/possession.

examples.english: There is Han River in Korea

patterns.meaning: Dual goodbye system: 계세요 (said by the person leaving, to the one staying) / 가세요 (said by the person staying, to the one leaving)

examples.english: Goodbye (stay safe - said to person staying)
Goodbye (go safely - said to person leaving)

patterns.meaning: Where are you going? (어디 = where, 에 = to/direction, 가요 = go)

patterns.pattern: 차가운 거 드릴까요? / 따뜻한 거 드릴까요?
patterns.meaning: Would you like a cold/hot one? (server asking about drink temperature)

examples.english: Would you like a cold one?
Would you like a hot one?

patterns.pattern: Vertical vowel (ㅏ,ㅓ,ㅣ) = right of consonant; Horizontal vowel (ㅗ,ㅜ,ㅡ) = below consonant
patterns.meaning: Hangul letter positioning rule: vowel shape determines consonant placement in syllable block
좋아요 = it's good (describes the object); 좋아해요 = I like it (describes speaker's attitude/action)

examples.english: This soup is good (about the soup)

patterns.meaning: When a syllable-final consonant (batchim) is followed by a vowel, it fills the empty consonant slot of the next syllable

examples.english: Eat (polite) - ㄱ batchim links to 어
Water one glass - ㄹ links to 한

patterns.pattern: Question = rising pitch (↑) / Statement = falling pitch (↓)
patterns.meaning: In Korean, questions and statements have identical word order. Only intonation distinguishes them.

examples.english: Do you go? / Are you going? (question)

patterns.meaning: Connects two actions: 'do X and then Y' or lists actions
Base/neutral/dictionary form of verb. Used in written language and for looking up words.
Conditional: 'if/when [action]'
Polite conversational form. Use 아요 when stem vowel is ㅏ or ㅗ; use 어요 for other vowels.

examples.english: Come (polite, irregular: 오+아요 contracts to 와요)

patterns.meaning: Contrastive connector: 'do X but...'
에 = direction TO a place; 에서 = FROM a place. Movement particles.
What do you [verb]? (뭐 = what, question word)
Where do you come from? (어디 = where, 에서 = from, 와요 = come)

patterns.pattern: Past tense: polite form + ㅆ + 어요
patterns.meaning: Converting present polite form to past tense by adding 쌍시옷 (ㅆ) and 어요
I went to [place]
I came from [place]

examples.english: I came from the United States

patterns.meaning: Where did/do [subject] [verb]? (direction)
Where did you come from?

examples.english: I came from the United States

patterns.pattern: Korean word order: Time → Place → With whom → Object → Action
patterns.meaning: Korean sentence structure starts from big environment and zooms into action (verb-final)

examples.english: Yesterday in Durham with friend pizza ate

patterns.meaning: I am [name]. Use 이에요 after consonant, 예요 after vowel.
Marks the subject or topic of the sentence
Marks the object of the sentence
I live in [place]
I lived in [place]
I work at [place]
Final ㄱ is unreleased; tongue touches roof of mouth without air puff
Marks movement TO a place or static existence AT a place
Marks where an action takes place (AT) or origin (FROM)
Ongoing action in progress
Past tense polite ending
Polite present tense ending; pronounced as single smooth 'yo' sound with rounded lips
I speak in [language]
I learn [language]
I listen to [noun]
I like [noun]
I regularly attend/go to [place]
I study [subject]
I want to ~ (change dictionary 다 to 고 싶어요)

examples.english: I want to go to Busan
I want to buy a plane ticket
I want to go to church
I want to eat a cookie

patterns.meaning: I am interested in ~
Identity or descriptive copula in polite form

patterns.pattern: ㅎ batchim + vowel → ㅎ drops (H-drop irregular)
patterns.meaning: When ㅎ at the bottom of a syllable meets a vowel, the ㅎ sound disappears
What do you want to [verb]?

examples.english: What do you want to buy?

patterns.meaning: Placed directly before a verb to negate it

examples.english: I don't want to receive it

patterns.meaning: Where do you want to go?

examples.english: Where do you want to go?
I want to go to Busan

patterns.meaning: Did you [verb] [noun] yesterday?

examples.english: Did you read a book yesterday?

patterns.meaning: Really/very [adjective/verb] (casual slang, using 개 as emphasis prefix)
Expresses future plans or intentions

examples.english: I will eat at Jagalchi Market
I will go to Busan in May
What will you do this weekend?

patterns.meaning: I will ~ (expressing intention/promise)
By means of / with (instrument particle)
May I ~? / Is it okay if I ~?
Present tense (polite) - do ~
Past tense (polite) - did ~
Went to ~ (past tense, location particle)

examples.english: I want to go to church

patterns.meaning: Where did you come from? / I came from ~
Where is ~?
Please give me / Please do (polite request)
Please do ~ for me (polite request with action verb)
B is more [adjective] than A

examples.english: I like tea more than coffee

patterns.meaning: Adjective modifying form placed before nouns
[Object] is cold/hot (object temperature description)
[Place/Weather] is cold/hot (weather temperature description)
Subject marker: 이 after consonant, 가 after vowel (used with descriptive adjectives)
Korean uses temperature adjectives metaphorically for personality traits
차갑다/뜨겁다 for objects (touch), 춥다/덥다 for weather/environment
Topic marker: 은 after consonant, 는 after vowel (used with action verbs)

examples.english: As for me, I went to the hospital yesterday

patterns.meaning: Cold/hot + noun (adjective modifier before noun)
Expresses desires or wishes; attach to verb stem

examples.english: What do you want to receive?
I want to renew my passport
I want to go to Korea
I want to become a teacher

patterns.meaning: Went to [place]. Did [action]. (Korean separates location and action into two sentences)

examples.english: I went to the hospital. I worked.
I went to the store. I shopped.
I went to the cafe. I drank coffee.

patterns.meaning: In Korean, separate the location and action into two sentences when describing past activities

examples.english: I went to the hospital. I worked.
I went to the store. I shopped.
I went to a cafe. I drank coffee.
I went to a restaurant. I ate.
I went to the library. I studied.

patterns.meaning: Use 에서 when describing where an ongoing/habitual activity happens
I work at ~ (location of activity with particle 에서)
Thank you for sharing ~ (polite conversation closing)

examples.english: Thank you for sharing your perspective
Thank you for sharing your information

patterns.meaning: Used when talking about getting gifts, calls, messages, or items from others
Not / don't (negation before verb)
Used to talk about current trends, feelings, or activities

examples.english: What do you like these days?
The weather is cold these days.

patterns.meaning: Indicates static location, destination, or time
Indicates origin (from) or location where an activity takes place
I live in ~ (current residence with particle 에)
I lived in ~ (past residence with particle 에)
I came from ~ (origin with particle 에서)
Nationwide + weather phenomenon
Cold + noun (shortened adjective modifier)

sentences.english: It's okay / I'm fine / No thank you
To-go please / Wrap it up please
I came from the United States

sentences.korean: 저는 카페에서 일해요. 도서관에 공부하러 가요.

sentences.korean: 안녕하세요. 저는 Chasity예요. Chapel Hill에 살아요. 방사선 종양학에서 일해요. 한국어 배워요. 만나서 반가워요!
sentences.english: Hello. I am Chasity. I live in Chapel Hill. I work in Radiation Oncology. I'm learning Korean. Nice to meet you!

sentences.korean: 안녕하세요. 저는 Chasity예요. Chapel Hill에 살아요. 방사선 종양학에서 일해요. 한국어 좋아해요! 5월에 부산에 갈 거예요. 만나서 반가워요!
sentences.english: Hello. I am Chasity. I live in Chapel Hill. I work in Radiation Oncology. I like Korean! I will go to Busan in May. Nice to meet you!
Welcome! What can I get you?
I will eat at Jagalchi Market.
I want to become a teacher.
I want to eat a cookie
That person is a bit of a cold person.
My mom is a warm person.
What will you do this weekend?
I like tea more than coffee.
I went to the store. I shopped.

sentences.korean: 갱신된 여권과 무제한 여행을 받고 싶어요.
sentences.english: I want to receive a renewed passport and unlimited travel.
Thank you for sharing your perspective
That kind of person learns Korean quickly too.
I went to the library. I studied.
I want to receive unlimited travel.
What do you want to receive?
I went to the hospital. I worked.
I ate biscuits and gravy. I also drank coffee.
She is a person who likes experiences more than gifts.
I want to use my time well.
I went to a restaurant. I ate.
I don't want to receive it.

sentences.korean: 안 받고 싶어요. 저는 그런 거 안 좋아해요.
sentences.english: I don't want to receive anything. I don't like that kind of thing.
You don't want to receive anything?
I want to renew my passport.
The weather is cold these days.

sentences.korean: 요즘 많은 사람들이 좋아하는 것을 물어보면 보통 가수나 밴드 이름을 말해요.
sentences.english: When you ask many people what they like these days, they usually say a singer or band name.
What do you like these days?

sentences.korean: 일관성은 매일 같은 노력을 하는 거예요.
sentences.english: Consistency is making the same effort every day.

sentences.korean: 일관성이 좋아요. 저는 생활 목표가 있어요. 시간과 재능을 잘 관리하고 싶어요.
sentences.english: I like consistency. I have life goals. I want to manage my time and talents well.
A person who knows their life goals.
I want to receive a gift.
Yesterday, I went to the hospital
I want to renew my passport.
Thank you for sharing your information
I want to become a good steward.
I went to the cafe. I drank coffee.

sentences.korean: 한국 사람들은 이런 사람을 '똑똑한 사람'이라고 해요.
sentences.english: Korean people call this kind of person a 'smart person.'
I want to go to Korea.
